verdicts.question: What type of roof is present at the site?
verdicts.photo_observation: Pages 7–10 (Material Storage and Proposed PV Area) all show a flat concrete roof surface with parapet walls and a staircase headroom structure. No slope or tiles visible anywhere on the roof.
verdicts.rationale: Multiple rooftop photos on pages 7–10 clearly confirm a flat RCC roof; entirely consistent with 'RCC Flat Roof'.

verdicts.question: Are there any obstructions on the roof that could affect the solar installation?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 11 (top) shows five satellite dishes (Airtel, TATA and others) mounted on a parapet wall of the roof. Pages 9–10 also show satellite dishes visible on roof parapets in adjacent sections. The proposed panel areas (blue overlays on pages 8–10) appear to be in open zones away from these dishes, but the dishes are present on the parapet edges and could cast shadows on panels installed nearby.
verdicts.rationale: Multiple satellite dishes on parapet walls are visible (page 11 top, pages 9–10) and could cause shadow effects on panels installed near those edges. While the marked proposed panel zones appear separate from the dishes, proximity to parapet-mounted dishes warrants manual verification before confirming 'No obstructions'.

verdicts.question: What is the angle of the panels? (in degrees)
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 17 (bottom) shows a 3D render of the ballast-mounted panels on the flat roof. The tilt appears very shallow — the panels are nearly flat with a gentle southward rise. Rear-edge height shown as 0.44 m over what appears to be a ~2 m run, implying roughly 12° geometrically; however, if the 0.44 m includes the ballast frame base height, the actual panel tilt would be lower and plausibly 7°.
verdicts.rationale: 3D layout render on page 17 confirms a very shallow tilt on a flat roof broadly consistent with 7–12°. The visual is consistent with a low-angle ballast installation; no contradiction of the 7° claim, and exact angle from a 3D render is uncertain.

verdicts.question: On how many roofs at the site are we installing solar?
verdicts.photo_observation: Site Layout – Top View on page 16 shows all solar panel arrays on one building's roof (an irregular multi-wing shape). Pages 8–10 show different camera angles of what appears to be the same continuous rooftop. The building complex (Midtown Raaga) presents as one interconnected structure sharing a single rooftop level.
verdicts.rationale: Site layout (page 16) places all panels on one building's roof. Multiple roof photo angles (pages 8–10) appear to be different sections of the same flat roof rather than separate buildings, consistent with '1 roof'.

verdicts.question: What type of mounting structure is planned?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 17 (bottom 3D render) shows panels resting on the flat roof surface with low-profile angled frames, consistent with a ballast/weight-based mounting system. No roof-penetrating anchors are visible. The flat RCC roof is the standard substrate for ballast installations.
verdicts.rationale: 3D render on page 17 shows low-profile frames on flat RCC roof without visible anchor penetrations, consistent with ballast mounting.

verdicts.question: What is the orientation of the solar panels?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 16 (Site Layout – Top View) includes a compass rose labeling North (up), South (down), East (right), West (left). The solar panel arrays are arranged on the roof with their low/front edge clearly pointing toward the south side of the diagram. The 3D perspective view on page 17 corroborates this south-facing tilt direction.
verdicts.rationale: Compass on the site layout (page 16) clearly shows panels tilted toward south, consistent with designer's 'South' orientation answer.

verdicts.question: How can the roof be accessed?
verdicts.photo_observation: Pages 5–6 explicitly show interior staircase photos under the 'Staircase Details' section header. A standard interior staircase with metal railing and wooden steps is visible. Page 6 notes the staircase width as 1 m.
verdicts.rationale: Staircase photos on pages 5–6 confirm roof access via internal staircase.

verdicts.question: Is the building currently under construction?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 18 (Building Exterior, Google Maps street view) shows a fully completed, painted multi-story residential building with glass windows, balconies, and a decorative entrance gate with the 'Midtown Raaga' Kannada nameplate. No bare brickwork, scaffolding, or signs of active construction are visible.
verdicts.rationale: Building exterior photo on page 18 shows a fully finished occupied building; no construction indicators present.

verdicts.question: What is the height of the building and how many floors does it have?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 18 (Building Exterior) shows the building facade. Counting visible window rows/floor levels: 4 distinct floor levels of windows are visible above the ground-floor entrance level, consistent with a 4-floor structure (G+3 upper floors). No additional floors or penthouse visible.
verdicts.rationale: Building exterior on page 18 shows approximately 4 floor levels of windows, consistent with '4th floors' description.

verdicts.question: Is the site photo matching with the building description?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 18 shows a Google Maps street view of Midtown Raaga with Kannada signage. The aerial render (page 5), rooftop photos, cable routing interior photos, and building exterior all consistently depict the same commercial residential complex (INDIQUBE SPACES LIMITED – MIDTOWN RAAGA) at the Kodigehalli address.
verdicts.rationale: All photos throughout the TSVR consistently depict the same building matching the project description.

verdicts.question: Is there an overhead HT line passing within a 10-meter radius of the site?
verdicts.photo_observation: Pages 8–11 show rooftop views and page 18 shows the building exterior. No overhead high-tension lines are visible in any photo within the immediate site vicinity. A telecom tower/antenna is faintly visible in the background of page 9 (top), but it appears to be a distance away and is not a power HT line.
verdicts.rationale: No HT lines visible in any rooftop or exterior photos. 10-meter radius cannot be precisely measured from photos, but no indication of HT lines present near the building.

verdicts.question: Can the delivery van/truck reach the material unloading point?
verdicts.photo_observation: Pages 12–14 show the basement parking area where a sedan car and motorcycles are parked, confirming vehicle access to the basement. The aerial render on page 5 shows a road and driveway running adjacent to the building complex.
verdicts.rationale: Basement parking with car access visible in photos (pages 12–14) and driveway apparent in aerial view (page 5) confirm vehicle access for delivery.

verdicts.question: Are there any obstacles at the site that could hinder material lifting?
verdicts.photo_observation: Pages 5–6 show the interior staircase with a noted width of 1 m. The NovaSys solar panels are 2.278 m long × 1.134 m wide. Carrying 2.278 m panels up a 1 m wide staircase would be physically challenging and likely requires tilting or a special technique. No other obvious obstacles are noted in photos.
verdicts.rationale: Staircase width of 1 m (page 6) is narrower than the 2.278 m panel length and may constitute a material-lifting obstacle. Despite the designer's 'No' answer, this warrants manual review.

verdicts.question: Where will the Inverter, DCDB, ACDB, Battery, etc. be located?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 16 (Site Layout – Top View) labels 'Inverter, ACDB and DCDB' at the top-left section of the building rooftop (terrace level). Page 11 (bottom, cable routing diagram) marks the inverter position near the staircase headroom on the rooftop. Page 15 (top, Equipment Location) shows a utility room with cylindrical tanks and purple overlay markers for equipment positions, which may be a terrace-level enclosed utility room.
verdicts.rationale: Site layout (page 16) and cable routing diagram (page 11) both confirm inverter/ACDB/DCDB placement at terrace level, consistent with 'It in the terrace'.

verdicts.question: Is a canopy required for protecting the Inverters/ACDB/DCDB?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 15 (Equipment Location) shows the proposed inverter area appears to be an enclosed indoor utility room with overhead lighting (recessed lights visible). If the inverter is in this sheltered room, no external canopy is needed. The staircase headroom structure on the terrace also provides structural shelter nearby.
verdicts.rationale: Equipment location appears to be in an enclosed room (page 15) providing inherent shelter; no external canopy required if equipment is housed indoors.

verdicts.question: Did you discuss the location of the earth pits with the customer?
verdicts.photo_observation: Pages 15–16 show the Earth Pit Location section with DC Earth Pit (blue), AC Earth Pit (green), and LA Pit (orange) all marked in a garden area with green lawn, trees, and a footpath along the building perimeter.
verdicts.rationale: Earth pit location photos (pages 15–16) clearly show all three pits planned in a green garden area with soil, directly consistent with 'garden Area'.

verdicts.question: Where is the Internet Router located?
verdicts.photo_observation: Pages 12–14 show the basement area including an Electrical Panel Room with cable infrastructure. The basement has visible electrical installations. No router unit is explicitly visible or labeled in any photo.
verdicts.rationale: No router is visible or labeled in any photo. Basement electrical infrastructure is confirmed, but the router specifically cannot be verified from available photos.

verdicts.question: What's the Wi-Fi strength of the router near to the String inverter/Envoy?
verdicts.photo_observation: The building is clearly completed and occupied (page 18). Wi-Fi signal strength at the rooftop inverter cannot be assessed from photos. The 'Not Available / Under Construction' answer is unexpected for a completed and occupied building — it may mean signal was not tested at the inverter location, or the data logger/router connection was not available during the visit.
verdicts.rationale: Wi-Fi signal strength is not verifiable from photos. The 'Not Available / Under Construction' answer for a clearly completed building is ambiguous and may warrant clarification with the designer.

verdicts.question: Did you pin the site location in your WhatsApp when you were at the site?
verdicts.photo_observation: GPS coordinates (13.004601, 77.730162) and a Google Maps link are present on the cover page (page 1). This is a process documentation item; the WhatsApp pin itself is not visible in site photos.
verdicts.rationale: GPS coordinates are provided on the cover page supporting the claim, but WhatsApp pin sharing is a process item not verifiable from site photos.

verdicts.question: What type of energy meter is currently installed?
verdicts.designer_answer: Phase: Three Phase, Type: Digital, If 3-phase: LT with CT (35 - 49.999)
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 6 (Meter Box section) shows the main meter labeled 'Main Meter' with '3N' (three-phase neutral) notation, 'CT-2(a)' reference, '100/5' CT ratio, and 'K=20' multiplier. The VICTORY brand meter shows R, Y, B, N phase voltage readings, confirming a three-phase digital LT meter with CT.
verdicts.rationale: Meter box photo on page 6 shows a three-phase digital meter with CT (100/5 ratio, 3N labeling, VICTORY brand), consistent with 'Three Phase, Digital, LT with CT'.

verdicts.question: Is the Meter cubicle photo matching with the previous answer?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 6 shows the meter cubicle with a three-phase digital CT meter (VICTORY brand, 100/5 CT, K=20). The photo clearly matches the answer of Three Phase Digital LT with CT meter.
verdicts.rationale: Meter cubicle photo on page 6 clearly shows a three-phase digital CT meter, matching the meter type described in Q1.

verdicts.question: Has the termination point in the Main meter panel been identified?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 12 (bottom) shows the 'Electrical Panel Room' label on a basement room. Color-coded cable routing overlays (orange, green, purple) show cables terminating at this room. Page 13 (top) shows the interior of the electrical panel area with cable routing arriving at it.
verdicts.rationale: Electrical Panel Room identified and labeled on page 12; cable routing terminating at it confirms the termination point has been identified.

verdicts.question: Is the Termination Point easily visible or sealed?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 13 (top) shows the electrical panel room interior viewed from outside a grill/iron-bar gate. Electrical equipment is partially visible through the grill, but no close-up of the specific termination point or busbar within the main panel is provided.
verdicts.rationale: No close-up photo of the termination point within the main panel is available. The panel room is accessible but the specific connection point cannot be confirmed as 'visible' vs 'sealed' from the available photos.

verdicts.question: Is a Lightning Arrestor (LA) already available at the site?
verdicts.photo_observation: No LA device is visible in any rooftop or electrical room photo. Pages 15–16 show a dedicated 'LA Pit' planned in the garden, indicating LA is to be newly installed rather than already present. No existing LA hardware spotted on rooftop, parapet, or electrical room.
verdicts.rationale: No existing LA visible in photos; LA pit provision for new installation confirms LA is not currently available, consistent with 'No'.

verdicts.question: Is space available for CT within Energy meter panel?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 6 meter photo shows '100/5' and 'K=10' annotations indicating CT components are already present or accounted for in the meter installation. The meter panel accommodates CT components.
verdicts.rationale: Meter photo on page 6 shows CT ratio markings (100/5) indicating CT is accommodated within the meter panel, consistent with 'Yes' space available.

verdicts.question: Provide details of the existing CT ratings (if applicable).
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 6 meter box photo shows '100/5' and 'K=10' written on the meter face/label. The designer's '100/' appears to be an incomplete entry of '100/5' (a CT ratio of 100:5 amperes with multiplier K=10).
verdicts.rationale: Photo on page 6 shows '100/5' CT ratio — the designer's '100/' is a truncated version of this. Photo confirms the CT rating.

verdicts.question: In Main meter panel, is empty space available for solar meter?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 6 shows the main meter panel as a compact CT meter enclosure with little apparent spare space. Page 7 shows a separate utility/storage room designated for solar meter installation (labeled 'Solar meter'), implying the solar meter cannot be accommodated in the main panel.
verdicts.rationale: Main meter panel (page 6) appears compact and fully occupied; separate solar meter location identified on page 7, consistent with 'No' space in main panel.

verdicts.question: Is there space available to dig DC, AC and LA earth pits?
verdicts.photo_observation: Pages 15–16 show DC Earth Pit, AC Earth Pit, and LA Pit all marked in a green garden area with visible soil, grass, and trees. Ample open ground space is clearly present for all three pits.
verdicts.rationale: Earth pit location photos (pages 15–16) confirm ample garden/soil space for all three pits (DC, AC, LA).

verdicts.question: Are there any challenges with respect to Installation and Maintenance activities?
verdicts.photo_observation: Rooftop is accessible via staircase (pages 5–6), roof is flat and open (pages 7–10), building is complete and operational. The 1 m staircase width (page 6) with 2.278 m panels is a potential panel-lifting consideration, but not typically classified as an installation/maintenance challenge once panels are on the roof.
verdicts.rationale: No significant installation or maintenance challenges visible. Flat accessible roof, complete building, accessible electrical room. Panel-lifting concern noted separately in General Q2.

verdicts.question: Are there any safety risks associated with accessing the roof for installation?
verdicts.photo_observation: Pages 5–6 show a standard interior staircase with proper metal railings. Rooftop photos (pages 7–10) show parapet walls on all visible roof edges providing fall protection. No unmarked openings, unstable surfaces, or missing railings are visible.
verdicts.rationale: Staircase has proper railings (pages 5–6) and rooftop has parapet walls for edge protection (pages 7–10); no safety risks apparent in photos.

verdicts.question: Is space present in rooftop to store the panels?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 7 (Material Storage) shows the rooftop with a staircase headroom structure wall and parapet walls. Pages 8–10 show the open rooftop with parapet walls around the perimeter. Adequate wall surfaces (parapet and staircase housing walls) are available to lean panels vertically during staging.
verdicts.rationale: Rooftop photos (pages 7–10) show parapet walls and staircase headroom walls suitable for vertically staging panels during installation.

verdicts.question: Is ventilation available in the battery location?
verdicts.photo_observation: No dedicated battery storage area is clearly identified in the photos. Page 15 (Equipment Location) shows a utility room with cylindrical tanks and planned inverter positions; no ventilation openings (windows or vents) are visible in that room. If the battery is co-located here, the absence of visible ventilation is consistent with the 'No' answer.
verdicts.rationale: Battery location is not explicitly identified or labeled in any photo. If co-located with inverters in the page 15 utility room, no ventilation is visible — consistent with 'No' — but the battery location itself is uncertain and warrants manual verification.

verdicts.question: Should the Inverters/ACDB/DCDB be installed on a metal grill or wall mount?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 15 (Equipment Location) shows the utility room where purple overlay markers indicate inverter/equipment placement against concrete/plastered walls. No metal grill structure is visible at the equipment location. The walls appear suitable for wall mounting.
verdicts.rationale: Equipment location utility room (page 15) shows concrete walls with equipment overlays against them; no grill structure present, consistent with 'Wall mount'.

verdicts.question: Provide the dimensions for solar meter cubicle placement (L x W of wall).
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 7 shows the solar meter location — a utility/storage room labeled 'Solar meter'. The room appears to be a medium-sized utility space but no measurements or scale references are provided in the photo to verify the 2 × 3 dimension claim.
verdicts.rationale: Solar meter room visible on page 7 but no scale references allow verification of the stated 2 × 3 m wall dimensions.

verdicts.question: Provide the dimension for earth pits location (L x W of available space).
verdicts.photo_observation: Pages 15–16 show the garden area with DC Earth Pit, AC Earth Pit, and LA Pit marked. The garden appears spacious with trees and lawn, but no measurement scale reference is present in the photos to verify the stated 2 × 4 m area.
verdicts.rationale: Earth pit garden area is confirmed (pages 15–16) and appears spacious, but the 2 × 4 m dimension cannot be verified from photos without scale references.

verdicts.question: Is LAN cable under customer scope?
verdicts.photo_observation: No LAN cable or network infrastructure is specifically shown or labeled in the site photos. This is a contractual/scope agreement question.
verdicts.rationale: LAN cable scope is a contractual matter not verifiable from site photos.

verdicts.question: Is there any shadow that affects the solar installation?
verdicts.photo_observation: Pages 8–11 show the proposed panel areas on the flat rooftop. Neighboring buildings visible in the background (pages 8–10) appear comparable in height or lower than this building. Satellite dishes on the parapet (page 11) could cast minor shadows on panels installed very close to those parapet edges, but the proposed panel zones appear to be in open sky areas. No tall trees or closely adjacent taller structures are visible.
verdicts.rationale: Rooftop photos show the proposed panel areas in open sky with no significant shadow-casting structures overhead. Neighboring buildings appear comparable or lower height; satellite dishes on parapets may cause minor edge shadows but are unlikely to significantly affect the main panel array at 7° tilt.

verdicts.question: Is there any trench work to be done to carry the AC cable to the meter location?
verdicts.photo_observation: Pages 12–14 clearly show the AC cable routing through the interior of the building: a wall penetration from rooftop level (page 12 top), conduits and cable trays along basement ceiling (pages 12–14), and routing to the Electrical Panel Room (page 12 bottom). All cable routing is internal through the building structure. No outdoor trenching is visible or implied.
verdicts.rationale: Cable routing photos (pages 12–14) confirm entirely internal routing through wall penetrations and ceiling cable trays with no outdoor trenching needed, consistent with 'No'.

verdicts.question: Is the cable routing in the site photos continuous and consistent across the documented run — from rooftop panels to DCDB to inverter to ACDB to solar meter to main panel — with no orphan ends, no unexplained mid-run size changes, and visible conduit / cable-tie / clipping where the run is exposed?
verdicts.designer_notes: No designer answer for this target — the vision routine verifies it by tracing cables across photos with 'Cable Routing', 'Equipment Location', 'Meter Box' and similar captions on pages 4+.
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 11 (Cable Routing): Rooftop cable routing shown with colored overlays (purple/red/green) from panel area toward staircase headroom (inverter location) — conduit sections visible on the flat roof. Page 12 top: Wall penetration through concrete wall showing multiple cables (orange, green, purple) in a partially conduit-covered chase passing from rooftop to building interior. Page 12 bottom: Internal cable routing inside basement with color-coded overlays reaching labeled 'Electrical Panel Room'. Pages 13–14: Continued basement routing through ceiling conduits/cable trays. The DC side (rooftop panels → DCDB → inverter on terrace) is documented only in layout diagrams (pages 16–17) but not in actual site photos. The inverter/ACDB → solar meter cubicle (page 7) → main meter (page 6) segment is shown in separate individual photos but the cable continuity between these points is not photographically traced in sequence.
verdicts.rationale: The AC cable run from rooftop wall penetration through the basement to the main panel room is traceable across pages 11–14 with no orphan ends and visible conduit/cable-tray containment. However, the DC side (panels to DCDB to inverter on terrace) is shown only in layout diagrams, not in actual photo evidence. The inverter-to-ACDB-to-solar meter-to-main panel chain is also not photographically traced end-to-end. Partial continuity confirmed for the building interior AC run; the rooftop DC segment and solar meter connection require manual verification.
